content.full_text:
Re:
Farn Por,rrrc.lr Pn.lcrrcrs CouurssloN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
June 1,2001
Charlotte Craven, Councilmember
City of Camarillo
Post Office Box248
Camarillo, CA 930 1 l-0248
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-224
Dear Ms. Craven
QUESTION
May you vote on a decision involving property located in a redevelopment area
which is adjacent to, and therefore within 500 feet of, your property if the specific
property involved in the decision is more than 500 feet from your property?
CONCLUSION
As a general rule, decisions directly affecting the real property of an offlrcial
require the official's disqualification. This includes decisions where the official's real
property is within 500 feet of property which is the subject of a governmental decision.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commissionregulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Pursuant to enclosed Regulation 18329(c)(3), informal assistance does not confer immunity.
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1 Since you are not
inquiring about a particular governmental decision, we are treating your request as one
for informal assistance.2 (Regulation 18329(bX8) )


File No. I-00-224
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are a city council member for the City of Camarillo. By virtue of your
membership on the city council, you are also a member of the Camarillo Community
Development Commission, the legislative body of the crty's Redevelopment Agency. At
the present time, there is a single project area in the city. You and your husband own two
parcels of residential property at 57159 and 65167 Raemere Street. These properties,
located within an "island" surrounded by the redevelopment area, are adjacent but not
included within the boundaries of the redevelopment plan.
All of the properties included within the boundaries of the project area are zoned
for commercial and industrial uses. At the present time, there are no developments
contemplated or underway, but there exists a potential for redevelopment activities in the
future. Because your land parcels are not located in the redevelopment area, your land
will never be the subject of a decision before the CCDC.
You are aware that you may be presumed to have a conflict of interest in
decisions involving the redevelopment project area since your land parcels are located
within 500 feet of the area's boundaries. Your question pertains to property within the
redevelopment area but more than 500 feet from your land parcels.
ANALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that public officials, whether elected or appointed, perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 of the Act
requires analysis of the following questions:3
Are you a'opublic official"?
As a member of the Camarillo Community Development Commission (CCDC),
you are a "member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government
agency" and, therefore, are subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
(Section 82048; Regulation 1 870 1 (a).)
3 These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at Regulation
18700(b). The Commission fact sheet "Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview" explains the steps of
this analysis and is enclosed for your information.


File No. l-00-224
Page No. 3
Are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
The Act provides the following:
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official,
actrng within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a
matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action,
or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Section 871 00; Regulation 187 02.1.)
a
a
a
A public official "participates in making a governmental decision"
when, actingwithin the authority of his or her position and without
significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the
governmental decision. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702 2.)
A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to
"influence a governmental decision" before his or her own agency if,
for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before
any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency.
(Section 87 1 00; Regulation 187 02.3.)
Under these provisions, you will "make a governmental decision" if you cast a
vote involving redevelopment decisions before the CCDC. Additionally, you will
"participate in making" or "influence" a governmental decision if you perform any of the
actions detailed above with respect to those decisions.
What are your economic interests - 
the possible sources of a financial conflict of
interest?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a."financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment a of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
4 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public offrcial, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the offrcial's immediate family, or tleir agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficial$ a lO-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family' is defined
at Section 82029 as an offlrcial's spouse and dependent children.


a
File No. l-00-224
Page No. 4
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d);
Regulation I 8703. 1(b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the
decision (Section 87 1 03 (c); Regulation 187 03 .3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within l2 months prior to the decision
(Section 87 1 03(e); Regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Sectio n 87 1 03 ; Regulation I 8703 . 5).
Assuming you have an investment of $2,000 or more in the property you own,
you have an economic interest in your land under the Act. )
Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the governmental
decision?
"Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if that real property is the subject of the
governmental decision, or if any part of that real property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
governmental decision." (Regulation 18704.2(a) ) Regulations 1870a.2@)(1) - (5)
(enclosed) describe circumstances where real property is the subject of the decision. If
any one of these provisions apply to your property, or to property located within 500 feet
of your property, your real property will be directly involved in the governmental
decision.6
5 You have inquired and provided facts only regarding your property adjacent to the
redevelopment area. For purposes of this letter, we assume that you have no other economic interests
relevant to decisions which may come before the CCDC.
u Under Step Five of the conflict-of-interest analysis, Fopefty which is directly involved in a
goverflmental decision is presumed to experience a material financial effect as a result of the decision,
absent proof to rebut this presumption. @egulation 18705.2(a)(l).) Where an economic interest of a
public official will experience such a material financial effect, the official may not participate in the
decision. (Sections 87100 and 87103.)


File No. I-00-224
Page No. 5
Please note that Regulation 1870a.2@)(4) specifically describes the subject of a
decision involving a redevelopment area.
"(4) The governmental decision is to designate the survey
area, to select the project area, to adopt the preliminary
plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the
environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan,
to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or
amend any of the above decisions; and real property in
which the official has an interest, or any part of it is located
within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
redevelopment area. For purposes of this subdivision, real
property is located 'within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the
subject of the governmental decision'if any part of the real
property is within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment project area."
Under this subdivision, even if a public official's property is not located within
the redevelopment area, where decisions affect an entire redevelopment area (e.g., the
adoption of a redevelopment plan, including certification of an environmental impact
report, approval of owner participation rules and adoption of the redevelopment plan by
ordinance of the city council), the distance between the public official's property and the
boundary of the redevelopment project area is the relevant measurement. (Havtkins
Advice Letter, No. A-95-026.) Since your property is adjacent to the redevelopment
area, you are within 5 00 feet of the area and are disqualified as to the types of decisions
described in Regulati on 187 04.2(aX+)
This conclusion is consistent with past advice where Commission staffadvised
that the distance should be measured from the official's property line to the nearest
boundary of the entire property if the decisions before the official affected an isolated site
within the property and were inextricably linkedT to the entire property. (Kaplan Advice
Letter, No. I-98-224; Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038; Ball Advice Letter, No. A-98-
124.)
' For example, inthe Libow Advice Letter, No. A-98-238, decisions regarding purchasing,
condemning, improving, leasing, etc. certain individual parcels of land within a shopping center stood to
affect the shopping center as a whole and not just the individual parcels to which they related. Commission
staff concluded that the property that would be considered the "subject" of these decisions would be the
shopping center as a whole, and not just the individual parcels to which the decisions related. In contrast, if
a redevelopment area has already been adopted and the city is now in the process of adopting specific
project area plans, these decisions may be able to be segmented in a manner which may permit you to
participate in some decisions. (Siegel Advice Letter, No. I-90-682.) For your convenience, we have
enclosed lheBall Advice Letter, No. A-98-124, whichaddresses segmentationthatmaybe applicableto a


File No. l-00-224
Page No. 6
Commission staff has previously advised, however, that where a decision affects
only a clearly defined, specific and isolated site, such as a specific building on a large
tract of land, the distance may be measured from the official's property line to that clearly
defined and specifically affected portion of the other property. (Kaplan, supra; Krauel
Advice Letter, No. I-92-11S.)8 In the Krauel letter, Councilmember McMillan owned
property within the specified regulatory distance of city-owned land. The city-owned
land consisted of the city hall, a local television studio, a public library, and a parking lot.
The possibly disqualifying city council decision concerned the public library. The
distance from Councilmember McMillan's property to the library site was greater than
the specified distance. The Commission advised that the greater distance was the proper
measure for that decision so long as the decision was limited to the library site but also
advised that if the decision concerned all the city-owned land, the shorter distance was to
be used.
As you can see, whether the distance is measured to the boundaries of the entire
redevelopment area or to the boundaries of only a smaller parcel of property within the
redevelopment area depends heavily on the type of decision before the official. As
previously mentioned, provisions of Regulations 18704.2(a)(t) - (5) must allbe analyzed
to assess if you will have a conflict of interest. If you would like assistance with this
analysis, please do not hesitate to contact our office once you have identified a particular
decision before the CCDC.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
I
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
NB.je
I :\Adviceltrs\O0224. doc
redevelopment decision before the CCDC. You may wish to write again if the CCDC proposes a method
of segmenting one or more decisions from a decision where a conflict exists.
t These letters all address the distance requirement which was previously found in Step 5
(Regulation 18705.2) of the conflict of interest analysis. For ease of application, this distance requirement
has been moved to Step 4 (Regulation 18704.2.) as part of the Commission's Phase 2 project.
,,0t
P.

QUESTION: May you vote on a decision involving property located in a redevelopment area
which is adjacent to, and therefore within 500 feet of, your property if the specific
property involved in the decision is more than 500 feet from your property?

CONCLUSION: As a general rule, decisions directly affecting the real property of an offlrcial
require the official's disqualification. This includes decisions where the official's real
property is within 500 feet of property which is the subject of a governmental decision.
2 Pursuant to enclosed Regulation 18329(c)(3), informal assistance does not confer immunity.
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").1 Since you are not
inquiring about a particular governmental decision, we are treating your request as one
for informal assistance.2 (Regulation 18329(bX8) )
